methods.description: Plants of 'Monroe' bean were grown under controlled greenhouse conditions to provide source material for pollen collection.

steps.description: Plants of 'Monroe' bean were grown in commercial planting medium (Fisons Sunshine mixture, Fisons Western, Vancouver, Canada) in 4.5-liter clay pots in a greenhouse at 25/21C (day/ night), with a coincident photoperiod of 14/10 hr (day/night).
steps.searchable_summary: cultivate bean plants in greenhouse under controlled conditions

methods.method_name: Pollen Collection and Protoplast Release Induction
methods.description: Freshly opened flower buds were collected, and pollen was subjected to various experimental solutions containing salts, boric acid, osmotic stabilizers, and adjusted pH, sometimes solidified with agar, to induce protoplast release.

steps.description: Freshly opened flower buds were collected between 0730 HR and 0930 HR.
steps.searchable_summary: collect fresh flower buds from bean plants

steps.description: Within 5 min of collection, pollen from each flower was shaken into the well of a 0.4 × 1.2-cm microcavity slide, followed immediately by the addition of two drops of an experimental protoplasm-release solution that contained various concentrations and combinations of NaCl, KCl, CaCl 2 , and H 3 BO 3 in glass-distilled water.
steps.searchable_summary: transfer pollen to slide and add experimental release solution
inputs: experimental protoplasm-release solution (various concentrations/combinations of NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, H3BO3 in water)
outputs: pollen sample on slide with release solution

steps.description: Slides were then placed in closed chambers at 25C and 98% RH.
steps.searchable_summary: incubate slides in controlled humidity chamber

steps.description: Osmotic stabilizers, sucrose (0.03 to 1.2 M), mannitol (0.03 to 1.2 M), and sorbitol (0.03 to 1.0 M), were included along with the protoplast-release solutions to evaluate their influence on both quantity and extent of release and on prolonged stability of the released protoplasts.
steps.searchable_summary: add osmotic stabilizers to protoplast release solutions

steps.description: The pH of the release solutions was adjusted between 5.0 and 9.0 using 1.0 N HCl or 1.0 N KOH.
steps.searchable_summary: adjust pH of protoplast release solutions

steps.description: In other trials, the same solutions were solidified with 0.5% or 1.0% agar.
steps.searchable_summary: solidify protoplast release solutions with agar

steps.description: Pollen was shaken onto the surface of agar medium in a 9.0 × 1.0-cm petri dish and covered with a drop of phloxine/methyl green dye (Owczarzak, 1952).
steps.searchable_summary: apply pollen to agar medium and add dye
outputs: pollen sample on agar with dye

methods.description: Microscopic observation and quantification of the extent of protoplast release from pollen grains after treatment with experimental solutions.

steps.description: The total number of pollen grains from which protoplasts were released and the number of those totally released from the cell wall were determined microscopically 5 min after pollen application.
steps.searchable_summary: microscopically count pollen grains with protoplast release (total and free)
outputs: count of total released pollen grains

steps.description: The extent of protoplasm release was recorded as: 1) total, the number of pollen grains showing protoplasm release (partial plus free); and 2) free ( Fig. lB), protoplasts totally removed from the cell wall.
steps.searchable_summary: categorize and record protoplast release extent metrics
inputs: count of total released pollen grains

steps.description: Each treatment mean included pollen from a minimum of 12 flowers picked at random, replicated three times, and repeated six times during a period of 30 days. At least 500 pollen grains were evaluated from each flower.
steps.searchable_summary: ensure minimum sample size and replication for protoplast release evaluation
inputs: experimental design parameters (flowers per treatment, replicates, repetitions, grains per flower)
outputs: evaluated data meeting sample size requirements

methods.description: Statistical procedures used to analyze the collected protoplast release data to determine significant differences between experimental treatments.

steps.description: All data were statistically evaluated by analysis of variance (Snedecor, 1956).
steps.searchable_summary: perform analysis of variance (ANOVA) on experimental data
inputs: protoplast release data (total, free) for all treatments and replicates

steps.description: treatment means were compared by LSD at P = 0.05 (Snedecor, 1956).
steps.searchable_summary: perform LSD post-hoc test for mean comparison at P=0.05
outputs: LSD test results (significant differences between means)

methods.description: Released protoplast viability was assessed using standard staining methods.

steps.description: Viability of released protoplasts was determined using methylene blue (Vairo, 1961).
steps.searchable_summary: assess protoplast viability using methylene blue stain

steps.description: Viability of released protoplasts was determined using fluorescein diacetate (Heslop-Harrison and Heslop-Harrison, 1970).
steps.searchable_summary: assess protoplast viability using fluorescein diacetate stain

methods.method_name: Enzyme Treatment and SEM Sample Preparation
methods.description: Pollen protoplasts were treated with cell wall-degrading enzymes, washed, centrifuged, and prepared for viewing using scanning electron microscopy to investigate physical alterations.

steps.description: pollen from each of 20 freshly opened bean flowers was shaken into the wells of two microcavity slides and covered with two drops of protoplast-release solution containing 0.03 M NaCl, 300 mg CaCl 2 / liter, 100 mg H 3 BO 3 /liter, and 0.29 M sucrose at pH 6.5.
steps.searchable_summary: prepare pollen samples on slides with standard release solution for enzyme treatment experiment
inputs: standard protoplast-release solution (0.03 M NaCl, 300 mg/L CaCl2, 100 mg/L H3BO3, 0.29 M sucrose, pH 6.5)
outputs: pollen samples on slides with standard solution

steps.description: After 5 rein, a drop of protoplasm-release solution containing 1% (w/ v) Rhozyme HP-150 (Rohm and Haas, Philadelphia), 1% (w/ v) cellulase R-10 and 1% (w/v) Macerozyme R-10 (both from Kinki Yokut Biochemical, Nishinomiya, Japan) was added to one slide and one drop of the protoplasm-release solution without enzymes was added to another slide.
steps.searchable_summary: add enzyme solution or control solution to pollen samples on slides
inputs: slides with pollen and standard solution (after 5 min incubation)
enzyme solution (1% Rhozyme HP-150, 1% cellulase R-10, 1% Macerozyme R-10 in release solution)

steps.description: After 15 min at 25C, pollen from each slide was washed with the same protoplast-release solution into separate 3-ml centrifuge tubes and centrifuged (4 min in a Sorvall GLC-1 with a relative centrifugal force of 45 × g).
steps.searchable_summary: incubate samples, wash pollen into tubes, and centrifuge

steps.description: The wash solution was poured off, and the washing procedure was repeated.
steps.searchable_summary: remove supernatant and repeat washing procedure
inputs: centrifuged tubes with pellet and supernatant

steps.description: A drop of solution containing washed protoplasts from enzyme-free or enzyme-treated solutions was frozen in N 2 slush and transferred under vacuum using a Hexland CT 1000A Cryo unit (East Challow, Oxfordshire, England) for viewing in a Hitachi S-530 scanning electron microscope.
steps.searchable_summary: freeze washed protoplasts using N2 slush and prepare for SEM using cryo unit

steps.description: viewing in a Hitachi S-530 scanning electron microscope.
steps.searchable_summary: view prepared sample using scanning electron microscope
